A judge of the dead 

"Minos's job after he got to the underworld was to be a judge of the dead. subject: communication
76.105.115.116.101.110.105.110.103.32.10.question_text: Listening is a six-part process that can be remembered through its acronym, the HURIER listening profile (Brownell, 1996) What are the six-parts of this profile?
76.105.115.116.101.110.105.110.103.32.10.answer_text:
Hearing: perceiving sounds and movements communicated by others verbally and nonverbally this also includes signs and gestures, such as when someone is communicating with American Sign Language.

Understanding: making sense of the verbal and Nonverbal cues that are being perceived, which requires undersstanding the words and language being spoken or signed

Remembering: storing the words in your memory long enough to further process what was said or signed

Interpreting: making meaning out of the combination of all the verbal and nonverbal messages that were conveyed during the interaction

Evaluating: making judgments about the message and deciding whether it is true or false, positive or negative, etc.

Responding: signaling that you have received the message and giving verbal and/or nonverbal feedback

83.111.108.118.101.32.115.101.99.40.952..question_text: Solve sec(θ) = 2cos(θ) to find the first four positive solutions
83.111.108.118.101.32.115.101.99.40.952..answer_text:
θ = π/4 , 3π/4, 5π/4, 7π/4
sec(θ) = 2cos(θ)
1 / cos(θ) = 2cos(θ)
1 = 2cos²(θ)
1/2 = cos²(θ)
√1/2 = cos(θ) # remember the square gives a + or - solution
cos(θ) = ±√1⁄2 = ± √2/2
cos⁻¹(±√1⁄2) = π/3 , 3π/4, 5π/4, 7π/4

"Antaeus is the son of the sea god Poseidon and the Earth goddess Gaea. He compelled all strangers who were passing through the country to wrestle with him. "Heracles is one of the most famous Greco-Roman legendary heroes. Heracles waged a victorious war against the kingdom of Orchomenus in Boeotia and married Megara, daughter of Creon, king of Thebes, but he killed her and their children in a fit of madness sent by Hera and, consequently, was obliged to become the servant of Eurystheus. 65.99.99.111.114.100.105.110.103.32.116..question_text: According to Hesiod's Works and Days, who possessed a jar containing misery and evil?
65.99.99.111.114.100.105.110.103.32.116..answer_text:
Pandora,

"In Greek mythology, Pandora is the first woman. In Hesiod’s Works and Days, Pandora had a jar containing all manner of misery and evil. Zeus sent her to Epimetheus, who made Pandora his wife. She afterward opened the jar, from which the evils flew out over the earth. Pandora’s jar became a box in the 16th century, when the Renaissance humanist Erasmus either mistranslated the Greek or confused the vessel with the box in the story of Cupid and Psyche."

Judgmental anchor refers to the person's current position on an issue.

latitude of acceptance: refers to positions your audience is already willing to agree with or support.

latitude of noncommitment: refers to positions your audience feels neutral or indifferent about

latitude of rejection: refers to positions your audience finds objectionable, and not willing to accept.

87.104.111.32.97.112.112.101.97.114.115..question_text: Who appears mainly as the messenger of the gods in the Odyssey, though in other literature he was associated with the protection of cattle and sheep?
87.104.111.32.97.112.112.101.97.114.115..answer_text:
Hermes,

"Hermes, son of Zeus and the Pleiad Maia, is often identified with the Roman Mercury and with Casmilus or Cadmilus, one of the Cabeiri. Both in literature and cult Hermes was constantly associated with the protection of cattle and sheep, and he was often closely connected with deities of vegetation, especially Pan and the nymphs. In the Odyssey, however, he appears mainly as the messenger of the gods and the conductor of the dead to Hades."

73.110.32.67.83.83.44.32.116.104.101.32..answer_text:
1. Any style rule marked !important by the reader (user)
2. Any style rule marked !important by the author
3. Style sheets written by the author
4. Style sheets created by the reader (user)
5. Browser's default style rules ("user agent style sheet")

83.111.108.118.101.32.116.97.110.40.120..question_text: Solve tan(x) = 3sin(x) for all solutions with 0 ≤ x < 2π (calculator to get final value)
83.111.108.118.101.32.116.97.110.40.120..answer_text:
x = 0, 1.231, π, 5.052 
Solution
tan(x) = 3sin(x) #Rewrite tan(x)
sin(x)/cos(x) = 3sin(x) # Now multiply to eliminate the fraction
sin(x) = 3sin(x)*cos(x) # don't divide by sin(x)
sin(x) - 3sin(x)*cos(x) # Factor out sin(x)
sin(x)(1-3cos(x)) = 0
sin⁻¹(0), π - sin⁻¹ = x # solutions for sin(x)
(1-3cos(x)) = 0 -> cos(x) = -1 / -3
cos⁻¹(1/3), -cos⁻¹(1/3) + 2π = x # solutions for cos(x)

subject: web_design
87.104.121.32.119.111.117.108.100.32.121.question_text: Why would you use the sandbox attribute with an iframe? Name the four values you can enter into this attribute.
87.104.121.32.119.111.117.108.100.32.121.answer_text:
The sandbox attribute is used with an <iframe> element to restrict what the embedded content can do. 

allow-scripts: allows the embedded content to run scripts
allow-same-origin: Allows the embedded content to be treated as being from the same origin as the containing document
allow-forms: Allows the embedded content to submit forms
allow-popups: Allows the embedded content to open new windows or tabs.

By default the iframe is unrestricted

65.99.99.111.114.100.105.110.103.32.116..question_text: According to Roman legend, which of the following creatures suckled Romulus and Remes after they were abandoned?
65.99.99.111.114.100.105.110.103.32.116..answer_text:
A Wolf

"The origins of Rome, as of all ancient cities, are wrapped in fable. The Roman fable is of Romulus and Remus, twin sons of Mars, abandoned on the flooding Tiber and deposited by the receding waters at the foot of the Palatine. Suckled by a she-wolf, they were reared by a shepherd and grew up to found Rome. (The bronze statue of the maternally ferocious wolf, now in the city’s Capitoline Museums, is one of the best-known works among the thousands of masterpieces in Rome.)"

subject: mythology
65.99.99.111.114.100.105.110.103.32.116..question_text: According to Greek Mythology, who captured Pegasus and rode him in his fight with the Chimera?
65.99.99.111.114.100.105.110.103.32.116..answer_text:
Bellerophon 

"The winged horse Pegasus sprang from the blood of the Gorgon Medusa as she was beheaded by the hero Perseus. With Athena’s (or Poseidon’s) help, another Greek hero, Bellerophon, captured Pegasus and rode him first in his fight with the Chimera and later while he was taking vengeance on Stheneboea (Anteia), who had falsely accused Bellerophon. Subsequently Bellerophon attempted to fly with Pegasus to heaven but was unseated and killed or, by some accounts, lamed. The winged horse became a constellation and the servant of Zeus."

To find the equation:

θ = 0.505 / 6π

73.110.32.71.114.101.101.107.32.97.110.1.question_text: In Greek and Roman mythology, who are the twin deities who aid shipwrecked sailors?
73.110.32.71.114.101.101.107.32.97.110.1.answer_text:
The Dioscuri (Castor and Pollux)

"The Dioscuri (Castor and Pollux) are twin deities who succour shipwrecked sailors and receive sacrifices for favourable winds. They are the children of Leda and either Zeus, the king of the gods, or Tyndareus, Leda’s mortal husband and the king of Lacedaemon. According to the usual version, Castor is the son of Tyndareus and thus is mortal, while Pollux is the son of Zeus (who famously had approached Leda in the form of a swan)."

83.111.108.118.101.32.51.115.101.99.178..question_text: Solve 3sec²(t) - 5sec(t) = 0 for all solutions with 0 ≤ t < 2π
83.111.108.118.101.32.51.115.101.99.178..answer_text:
3sec²(t) - 5sec(t) = 0
Factor
(3sec(t)+1)(sec(t)-2)
3sec(t) + 1 = 0 and sec(t) -2 = 0
sec(t) = -1/3 and sec(t) = 2/1
1/cos(t) = -1/3 and 1/cos(t) = 2/1
cos(t) = -3 and cos(t) = 1/2
inverse function to get the solutions
arccos(-3) = t is invalid: so no solutions
arccos(1/2) = t yield result π/3 and 5π/3

73.110.32.71.114.101.101.107.32.109.121..question_text: In Greek mythology, who fell in love with his own reflection in the waters of a spring?
73.110.32.71.114.101.101.107.32.109.121..answer_text:
Narcissus,

"In Greek mythology, Narcissus, the son of the river god Cephissus and the nymph Liriope, was distinguished for his beauty. According to Ovid’s Metamorphoses, Book III, Narcissus’s mother was told by the blind seer Tiresias that he would have a long life, provided he never recognized himself. However, his rejection of the love of the nymph Echo or (in an earlier version) of the young man Ameinias drew upon him the vengeance of the gods. He fell in love with his own reflection in the waters of a spring and pined away (or killed himself); the flower that bears his name sprang up where he died."

84.104.101.32.104.111.117.114.115.32.111.question_text: The hours of daylight in Seattle oscillate from a low of 8.5 hours in January to a high of 16 hours in July (www.mountaineers.org/seattle/...ylightHrs.html). When should you plant a garden if you want to do it during a month where there are 14 hours of daylight?
84.104.101.32.104.111.117.114.115.32.111.answer_text:
First we need an equation to solve, since we have the high and low points, and an indicator January of when the graph starts. We can get an equation.
We start in January with a low point of 8.5
so: ?-cos(?θ)+? : theta being time
midline is 8.5 + amplitude = 12.25
amplitude is the range / 2 = 3.75
period is 12 months or 12 = 2π/b -> B =π/6
solving for 14 hours
14 = -3.75cos(π/6 * θ)+12.25
(14 - 12.25) / -3.75 = cos(π/6 * θ)
cos⁻¹(-1.75 / 3.75) = π/6 * θ
both solutions are 
-cos⁻¹(-1.75/3.75) + 2π, cos⁻¹(-1.75 / 3.75) = π/6 * θ
divide by π/6 on both sides to get both values of t
t₁ to get the month (3.927 months past january)

73.110.32.71.114.101.101.107.32.109.121..question_text: In Greek mythology, who was the mother of Artemis and Apollo?
73.110.32.71.114.101.101.107.32.109.121..answer_text:
Leto 

"Leto was the mother of Artemis and Apollo. In Greek mythology, she was a Titan goddess and the daughter of the Titans Coeus and Phoebe. Leto was known for her beauty and was pursued by Zeus, resulting in her becoming pregnant with his children. However, Hera, Zeus's wife, was jealous and made it difficult for Leto to find a place to give birth. Eventually, Leto found the island of Delos where she gave birth to Artemis and Apollo. Therefore, Leto is the correct answer as she is the mother of both Artemis and Apollo."

73.110.32.71.114.101.101.107.32.109.121..answer_text:
Echidna 

"In Greek mythology, Typhon, a monstrous serpentine giant and one of the deadliest creatures, is often said to be the mate of Echidna. Echidna is a hybrid creature, typically depicted as a serpent or dragon with the upper body of a woman. Together, Typhon and Echidna became the parents of several other monstrous beings, including the Sphinx, Cerberus, and the Chimera. Echidna is sometimes referred to as the "Mother of All Monsters" due to her role in giving birth to many of the most fearsome creatures in Greek mythology."

12 titans,

"In Greek mythology, a Titan is any of the children of Uranus (Heaven) and Gaea (Earth) and their descendants. "Aphrodite is the Greek goddess of sexual love and beauty, identified with Venus by the Romans. 65.32.99.97.98.108.101.32.116.104.97.116.question_text: A cable that anchors the center of the London Eye Ferris wheel to the ground must be replaced. What is the angle from the ground up to the center of the Ferris wheel? and how long is the cable?
65.32.99.97.98.108.101.32.116.104.97.116.answer_text:
tan(θ) = 70/23 
so the angle is θ = tan⁻¹(70/23)
the length of the cable can be found with the pythagoreum theorum
length = √(70²+23²)

71.83° and 73.68m long

87.104.97.116.32.97.114.101.32.102.111.1.answer_text:
1. Acknowledge - your audiences everyday undestanding, or "lay" theory, of how the phenomenon works. Consider asking your audience to describe the concept in their own words to better understand their perspective
2. Acknowledge - Why that explanation seems plausible on the surface
3. Explain - why that impression is incorrect or incomplete, show examples that demonstrate why it is not adequate.
4. Explain - the concept or theory more completely and illustrate why it is effective.

subject: mathematics
87.104.97.116.32.105.115.32.116.104.101..question_text: What is the process to solve a sinusoidal function?
87.104.97.116.32.105.115.32.116.104.101..answer_text:
1. Isolate the trig function on one side of the equation
2. Make a substitution for the inside of the sin, cosine, or tangent (trig function)
3. Use inverse trig functions to find one solution
4. Use symmetries to find a second solution on one cycle (when a second exists)
5. Find additional solutions if needed by adding full periods
6. Undo the substitution

subject: mythology
73.110.32.71.114.101.101.107.32.97.110.1.question_text: In Greek and Roman legend, who is gifted the power to transform all that he touches into gold?
73.110.32.71.114.101.101.107.32.97.110.1.answer_text:
King Midas of Phyrgia,

"Midas, in Greek and Roman legend, is a king of Phrygia, known for his foolishness and greed. According to the myth, Midas found the wandering Silenus, the satyr and companion of the god Dionysus. For his kind treatment of Silenus Midas was rewarded by Dionysus with a wish. The king wished that all he touched might turn to gold, but when his food became gold and he nearly starved to death as a result, he realized his error. Dionysus then granted him release by having him bathe in the Pactolus River (near Sardis in modern Turkey), an action to which the presence of alluvial gold in that stream is attributed."

subject: mythology
73.110.32.71.114.101.101.107.32.109.121..question_text: In Greek mythology, How does King Minos die?
73.110.32.71.114.101.101.107.32.109.121..answer_text:
Boiled in a burning Bath,

"King Minos died by being boiled in a burning bath. This is a reference to the myth of King Minos from Greek mythology. According to the myth, King Minos was the ruler of Crete and had a labyrinth built to imprison the Minotaur, a half-human, half-bull creature. However, King Minos offended the gods, and as a punishment, his wife Pasiphae fell in love with the Minotaur and gave birth to its offspring. In retaliation, the gods caused King Minos to be scalded to death in a boiling bath."

68.101.115.99.97.114.116.101.115.32.79.1.answer_text:
- Things that depend on nothing for existence = Only God
- Things that depend on ONLY God for existence =  Material Substance & Mind/Soul
- Things that depend on other substance for existence = Properties
- Main property of MIND (immaterial substance) = Thought
- Main property of Matter (Material Substance) = Extension

subject: mathematics
83.111.108.118.101.32.50.115.105.110.178.answer_text:
cos(t) + 2 = 0 has no solutions:
2cos(t) -1 = 0 at t = π/3 , 5π/3

Solution:
2sin²(t) = 3cos(t)
2(1-cos²(t) = 3cos(t)
2 - 2cos²(t) - 3cos(t) = 0
-2cos²(t) - 3cos(t) + 2 = 0
2cos²(t) + 3cos(t) - 2 = 0
2cos²(t) + 4cos(t) - cos(t) - 2 = 0
2cos(t)(cos(t)+2) - 1(cos(t) + 2) = 0
(cos(t)+2)(2cos(t) -1) = 0
cos⁻¹(-2) = t AND cos⁻¹(1/2) = t
arccos(1/2) = π/3 and 5π/3

87.104.111.32.105.115.32.116.104.101.32..answer_text:
Hephaestus,

"According to myth, Hephaestus is the Greek god of fire. According to myth, Hephaestus was born lame and was cast from heaven in disgust by his mother, Hera, and again by his father, Zeus, after a family quarrel. He was brought back to Olympus by Dionysus and was one of the only gods to have returned after exile. subject: mathematics
73.110.32.97.32.118.105.100.101.111.32.1.question_text: In a video game design, a map shows the location of other characters relative to the player, who is situated at the origin, and the direction they are facing. A character currently shows on the map at coordinates (-3, 5). If the player rotates counterclockwise by 20 degrees, then the objects in the map will correspondingly rotate 20 degrees clockwise. Find the new coordinates of the character
73.110.32.97.32.118.105.100.101.111.32.1.answer_text:
we can use the equation x = r * cos(θ) and the pythagoreum theorum to get the current radius of the circle. Then add 20° to θ then reenter theta and solve for x.

-3 = √(9+25) * cos(θ)
-3 / √(34) = cos(θ)
θ = cos⁻¹(-3 / √34)

A counter-clockwise rotation would decrease the relative angle so 
x = √34 * cos(θ-20)
y = √(34-x²)
or
y= √34 * sin(θ-20)

83.116.101.112.115.32.116.111.32.102.97..question_text: Steps to factor a polynomial are? Given x²-2x-3 and 2x²-12x+11?
83.116.101.112.115.32.116.111.32.102.97..answer_text:
1. If leading coefficient (a) ≠ 1 factor out 1st 2 terms
2. Take 1⁄2 middle term, square term add it into parentheses and then balance.
3. Write ( ) as something squared use 1⁄2 middle term add to x²
Examples:
y = x²-2x-3
y = 1(x²-2x) -3
y = 1(x²-2x+1)-3 -1
y = 1(x²-2x+1)-4
y = 1(x-1)²-4
Example2:
y = 2x²-12x+11
y = 2(x²-6x+3²)+11-(3²*2)
y = 2(x²-6x+9) +11 - 18
y = 2(x²-3)²-7

subject: philosophy
87.104.97.116.32.105.115.32.97.32.84.101.question_text: What is a Teleological Argument for God? And What is Hume's critique of this kind of argument?
87.104.97.116.32.105.115.32.97.32.84.101.answer_text:
Teleological Argument: Order implies a designer; Hume criticizes analogy's validity

The Teleological Argument posits that the order and purpose observed in the universe suggest an intelligent designer, often identified as God. Hume critiques this argument by challenging the analogy drawn between human creations and the universe, questioning the validity of inferring a designer based on the limited scope of human understanding and experience.

87.104.97.116.32.105.115.32.116.104.101..question_text: What is the logical fallacy known as "sentimentality"?
87.104.97.116.32.105.115.32.116.104.101..answer_text:
The rhetorical fallacy of using excessive or manipulative evocation of positive emotional appeal.

Words commonly applied to sentimental appeals are "tear-jerking", "corny", and "sappy". Staples of sentimentality are religiosity (a religious posture without any commitment to substantial religious morality), flag-waving, images of Mom and apple pie, cute little children and puppy dog, soap-opera-like appeal to pity, and so on.

subject: philosophy
72.111.119.32.100.111.101.115.32.72.117..question_text: How does Hume suggest that belief in causality is formed?
72.111.119.32.100.111.101.115.32.72.117..answer_text:
Hume suggests that belief in causality is formed through custom and mental habit, based on the regularity of observed conjunctions between events. 

Hume argues that our belief in causality is not derived from rational inference but is a result of custom and mental habit. We observe events occurring in a regular sequence, such as the sun rising every morning, and come to expect this regularity to continue in the future. This habitual expectation forms the basis of our belief in causality, as we mentally associate certain events with others due to their constant conjunction. However, Hume emphasizes that this belief is not based on direct perception of causation but on the repeated experience of events occurring together.

87.104.97.116.32.105.115.32.116.104.101..answer_text:
The rhetorical fallacy of changing the sense in which a word is used, in the middle of an argument, or of using a definition of it that is not applicable in the context.

subject: philosophy
87.104.97.116.32.100.111.101.115.32.72.1.question_text: What does Hume mean by suggesting that ideas such as "Soul" or "Goodness" may be dubious or bogus?
87.104.97.116.32.100.111.101.115.32.72.1.answer_text:
Hume suggests that concepts like "Soul" or "Goodness" may be questionable because they have little correspondence to sensory impressions, making them potentially fictitious or imaginary. 

Hume's empiricist philosophy leads him to doubt the validity of ideas that cannot be traced back to sensory experience. Concepts such as "Soul" or "Goodness" are abstract and lack direct sensory impressions, leading Hume to question their basis in reality.

subject: philosophy
68.101.102.105.110.101.32.116.104.101.32.question_text: Define the view of Strategic Essentialism, in regards to Gender Essentialism
68.101.102.105.110.101.32.116.104.101.32.answer_text:
The view that a group is NOT metaphysically distinct, and yet there are strategic social reasons for a marginalized group to stress distinctness and shared identity in a struggle for equal rights. 

Example: Someone might not think there is one kind of essence that defines a Woman, or a Queer person, or an Indigenous Person, and yet it can be politically important to stress distinctiveness as a group to (1) foster political unity and (2) more clearly communicate the nature of their marginalization.

subject: philosophy
72.111.119.32.100.111.101.115.32.72.117..question_text: How does Hume characterize the role of custom in human understanding, and how does it differ from the role of reason?
72.111.119.32.100.111.101.115.32.72.117..answer_text:
Hume argues that custom, or repeated experience, is the foundation of human understanding, particularly in matters of fact. Custom allows us to form beliefs about the world based on repeated observations, while reason is limited in its ability to provide knowledge beyond what is directly experienced.

Hume emphasizes the fundamental role of custom in shaping our beliefs and expectations about the world. He suggests that our understanding of matters of fact, such as the regularity of the world, is based on custom rather than reason. While reason is important for abstract reasoning and logical deduction, it is custom that allows us to make sense of the world around us and predict future events.

subject: mathematics
70.105.110.100.32.116.104.101.32.101.120.question_text: Find the exact value of cos(75°)
70.105.110.100.32.116.104.101.32.101.120.answer_text:
Since 75° = 30°+45°, we can evaluate cos(75°) as:
cos(75°) = cos(30°+45°)
Apply the cosine sum of angles identity
cos(30°+45°) = cos(30)cos(45) - sin(30)sin(45)

subject: mathematics
65.32.99.97.110.110.111.110.98.97.108.10.question_text: A cannonball is fired with velocity of 100 meters per second. If it is launched at an angle of θ, the vertical component of the velocity will be 100sin(θ) and the horizontal component will be 100 cos(θ). Ignoring wind resistance, the height of the cannonball will follow the equation h(t) = -4.9t² + 100sin(θ)t and horizontal position will follow the equation x(t) = 100cos(θ)t. If you want to hit a target 900 meter away at what angle should you aim the cannon.
65.32.99.97.110.110.111.110.98.97.108.10.answer_text:
To hit the target 900 meters away, we want x(t) = 900 at the time when the cannonball hits the ground, when h(t) = 0. To solve this problem, we will first solve for the time, t, when the cannonball hits the ground. Our answer will depend upon the angle θ
h(t) = 0
-4.9t²+100sin(θ)t) = 0
t(-4.9t+100sin(θ)) = 0 ---- Factor here
t = 0 or 4.9t + 100sin(θ) = 0
t = 100sinθ / 4.9 ---- solve for t, since 0 twice use the second value
x(t) = 900
100cos(θ)*(100sin(θ)/4.9) = 900
100²/4.9 * cos(θ) * sin(θ) = 900
cos(θ)sin(θ) = 900 / (100²/4.9)
2cos(θ)sin(θ) = 2 * (900 / 100²/4.9) --- multiply by two on both sides to take advantage of our identity
sin(2θ) = 8820/10000
2θ = sin⁻¹(8820/10000) == 2θ/2 = sin⁻¹(8820/10000)/2
θ = 1.080/2 = 0.54 = 30.94°

72.111.119.32.100.111.101.115.32.72.117..answer_text:
Hume's view suggests that all thoughts are combinations of impressions, while Locke proposed that combination might occur through mere chance. 

Hume rejects the idea that thoughts are randomly combined, as Locke suggested. Instead, he argues that there are specific principles of connection, such as resemblance, contiguity, and cause and effect, that govern how impressions and ideas are combined to form thoughts.

87.104.97.116.32.105.115.32.116.104.101..answer_text:

87.104.97.116.32.105.115.32.116.104.101..question_text: What is the rhetorical fallacy, a "quotation out of context"?
87.104.97.116.32.105.115.32.116.104.101..answer_text:
The rhetorical fallacy of quoting a few words or sentences from a source text in a manner that makes them appear to have a different meaning than they have within the context of the complete text. 

This is a common tactic in writers of invective who deliberately distort their opponents' ideas in this manner. It is also used by advertisers of cultural productions to put the most favorable spin on journalistic reviews, as when an ad quotes a review of a movie calling it "spectacular," when the full text reads, "This film is the most spectacular disaster in years."

87.104.97.116.39.115.32.116.104.101.32.1.question_text: What's the difference between class attributes and instance attributes in Python?
87.104.97.116.39.115.32.116.104.101.32.1.answer_text:

"Class attributes in Python are attributes that have the same value for all instances of a class. You must always assign them an initial value. When an instance of the class is created, class attributes are automatically created and assigned to their initial values.Instance attributes, on the other hand, are attributes whose value is specific to a particular instance of the class. They’re usually created and assigned in the .__init__() method."

72.111.119.32.100.111.101.115.32.72.117..question_text: How does Hume use the analogy of a mirror to explain the relationship between thoughts and objects?
72.111.119.32.100.111.101.115.32.72.117..answer_text:
Hume describes thoughts as a mirror that faithfully copies objects but represents them with faint and dull colors compared to the original perceptions. 

Hume uses this analogy to illustrate that while our thoughts accurately reflect the objects of our perceptions, they do so with less vividness and clarity than the original sensory experiences. This emphasizes the idea that our ideas are derived from our impressions but are not as vivid or immediate as the impressions themselves.

70.105.108.108.32.105.110.32.116.104.101.question_text: Fill in the blank, In Python, an object is considered `___________` if it can be altered dynamically.
70.105.108.108.32.105.110.32.116.104.101.answer_text:
mutable

"In Python, an object is considered mutable you can alter it dynamically.

This means that you can change its state or contents at runtime. Lists and dictionaries are examples of mutable objects.

On the other hand, strings and tuples are examples of immutable objects, which means that you can’t change their state or content after creating them.

This is an important concept to understand when working with different types of data in Python. For example, when you create a custom class, instances of that class are mutable by default. This means that you can change their attributes dynamically."

72.111.119.32.100.111.101.115.32.72.117..question_text: How does Hume's view on clarifying idea differ from traditional views?
72.111.119.32.100.111.101.115.32.72.117..answer_text:
Hume suggests that the best way to clarify ideas is to break them down into their component impressions, contrasting with traditional views that may emphasize rational analysis or abstract reasoning. 

Hume's approach emphasizes the importance of sensory experience in understanding concepts. He argues that by breaking down ideas into their component impressions, we can gain a clearer understanding of their origins and meaning. This contrasts with views that prioritize abstract reasoning or rational analysis as the primary method of clarifying concepts.

87.104.97.116.32.116.104.114.101.101.32..question_text: What three forms does the appeal to authority logical fallacy take?
87.104.97.116.32.116.104.114.101.101.32..answer_text:
1. Citing as a source on a particular subject someone who is an authority on some subject but not the one at issue. (The celebrity endorsement)
2. Citing the opinion of a source who is an authority on the issue as sufficient in itself, without presenting the evidence on which that opinion is based.
3. When a genuine authority is cited, but the authority's opinion diverges from the opinion of other authorities or is otherwise suspect.
3a. To avoid this third form of the fallacy, you should acknowledge the difference and present a case for why this authority's word should prevail over others' if you think it should.

A. Our senses have fooled us on multiple occasions. There is no way to tell that they are not fooling us now. So, we should exercise some level of skepticism regarding empirical knowledge and take it as only probable. 

B. Our senses have fooled us on multiple occasions. There is no way to tell that they are not fooling us now. So, we should take a methodological position of total skepticism and reject all sensory information as false

73.110.32.116.104.101.32.77.101.100.105..question_text:
In the Meditations, René Descartes decides to reject as false “all the opinions he has formerly accepted”, even if he does not know for sure that they are indeed false. Among the following,which is the best formulation of the justification he gives for his decision? 

A. Descartes is investigating whether there is any instance of knowledge that is absolutely certain. Since he recognizes that he has accepted all his current opinions by relying on unreliable sources, these opinions ought to be taken as false in the context of this particular investigation. 

B. Descartes is investigating whether there is any instance of knowledge that is absolutely certain. Looking at each of his opinions’ content, he has found out that all of them are false.

C. Descartes is looking to provide a true foundation for Modern science. Since he recognizes that he has accepted a lot of his opinions on the basis of Aristotle’s views,these opinions ought to be taken as false because they could conflict with the new science. 

D. Descartes is looking to provide a true foundation for Modern science. Since he takes knowledge to come from the impressions we get from our sensations and reflections, he decides to start with a blank slate.

subject: philosophy
72.111.119.32.100.111.101.115.32.72.117..question_text: How does Hume compare his view of ideas as copies of impressions to Plato's concept of Forms?
72.111.119.32.100.111.101.115.32.72.117..answer_text:
Hume contrasts his view with Plato's by suggesting that while Plato believed that physical objects are copies of Forms (or Ideas), Hume argues that our thoughts (ideas) are copies of our sensory impressions. 

Plato's theory of Forms posits that physical objects are imperfect copies of transcendent Forms, which represent the true reality. In contrast, Hume suggests that our ideas are copies of our sensory impressions, meaning that our thoughts reflect our direct experiences rather than an abstract, perfect reality.

67.104.101.99.107.32.111.117.116.32.116..question_text:
Check out the following class definition:

class Dog:
    species = "Canis familaris"
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name
        self.age = age

Which of the following statements about the Dog class are true?
1. To create new instances of Dog, you have to manually pass a value to the self parameter
2. Instantiating this class raise a TypeError every time
3. The .species attribute has the same value for every class instance
4. To create new instances of Dog, you have to manually pass values to the name and age parameters
67.104.101.99.107.32.111.117.116.32.116..answer_text:
3 & 4 are true

"The class attribute .species already has an initial value, which will be the same for every instance of this class.

To instantiate objects of this Dog class, you need to provide values for the name and age parameters. If you don’t, then Python raises a TypeError.

However, you don’t need to provide a value for the self parameter, because when you instantiate a Dog object, Python creates a new instance and passes it to the first parameter of .__init__()."

subject: philosophy
68.101.102.105.110.101.32.116.104.101.32.answer_text:
The view that objects have a set of attributes that are necessary to their identity. 

Further:
It’s easy to see how Rationalism lends itself to Essentialism. Plato and Descartes are the bestexamples. If we can know different KINDS of things by reason alone, then those differences in KIND are very rigid, perhaps even eternal truths. Like, a triangle is forever and always a different kind of thing than a square. A Mind is forever and always essentially different than a body.But Empiricists can be essentialists too. Carl Linnaeus, father of biological taxonomy, was a pretty firm essentialist. Now, his strict essentialism (some argue) cannot be empirically defended.That doesn’t stop it from remaining the foundational metaphysical presumption in centuries ofempirical studies. (lol)But even a less strict, empiricist essentialism might hope that human categories (our every day divisions and groupings of the world around us) might express something true about reality. They might still “Carve nature at its joints.” (from Plato’s Phaedrus, but a surviving aspiration of empiricism)

The Artemesium was famous not only for its great size, over 350 by 180 feet (about 110 by 55 metres), but also for the magnificent works of art that adorned it. The temple was destroyed by invading Goths in 262 ce and was never rebuilt."
- Encyclopedia of Britannica

65.99.99.111.114.100.105.110.103.32.116..question_text: According to Hume, what are the principles of connections? How does this help explain complex thoughts?
65.99.99.111.114.100.105.110.103.32.116..answer_text:
Hume suggests that complex thoughts are formed through principles of connection such as resemblance, contiguity, and cause and effect, which link various impressions and ideas in our minds. 

Hume argues that our thoughts are combinations of impressions and ideas that are connected through these principles. For example, the idea of "coffee is good" may be formed through the connection of the impression of tasting coffee with the feeling of pleasure, which is then associated with the concept of "goodness."

65.99.99.111.114.100.105.110.103.32.116..question_text: According to Hume, why can't inductive reasoning and belief in causality be justified rationally?
65.99.99.111.114.100.105.110.103.32.116..answer_text:
Hume argues that inductive reasoning and belief in causality cannot be justified rationally because they result from custom and mental habit, rather than direct perception of causation. 

Hume suggests that while we may observe events occurring in conjunction, such as one event following another, we do not actually perceive any causal connection between them. Our belief in causality is based on the regularity of these observed conjunctions, leading to a habitual expectation of certain events following others. This habitual expectation forms the basis of inductive reasoning, where we infer that future events will resemble past events. However, this inference is not based on rational justification but on the assumption that the future will resemble the past, a presupposition that cannot itself be grounded in prior experience.

subject: philosophy
87.104.97.116.32.100.111.101.115.32.72.1.question_text: What does Hume mean by the problem of induction?
87.104.97.116.32.100.111.101.115.32.72.1.answer_text:
The "problem of induction" refers to the difficulty in justifying the inference of causal relationships from past experience, as it relies on the assumption that the future will resemble the past. 

Hume's "problem of induction" arises from the observation that we can never directly perceive causality but only the constant conjunction of events. This means that to draw any causal inferences from past experience, we must presuppose that the future will resemble the past. However, this presupposition is itself a metaphysical assumption that cannot be grounded in prior experience. Therefore, the problem of induction highlights the challenge of justifying our belief in causal relationships based on observed regularities in nature.

87.104.97.116.32.105.115.32.116.104.101..answer_text:

72.111.119.32.100.111.101.115.32.72.117..question_text: How does Hume explain the basis of our assumptions about the regularity of the world?
72.111.119.32.100.111.101.115.32.72.117..answer_text:
Hume argues that our assumptions about the regularity of the world, which underlie the process of induction, cannot be rationally justified and must be derived from a non-rational part of our human nature, specifically from custom or habit.

Hume's problem of induction challenges the rational basis for our belief in the uniformity of nature. He suggests that our expectation that the future will resemble the past is not based on reason or evidence but on custom or habit. This means that our belief in the regularity of the world is not the result of rational inference but of a psychological tendency to expect things to continue as they have in the past.

subject: mathematics
83.111.108.118.101.32.50.115.105.110.178.question_text: Solve 2sin²(t)-cos(t) = 1 for all solutions with 0 ≤ t < 2π
83.111.108.118.101.32.50.115.105.110.178.answer_text:
Solution: t = π/3 or t = 5π/3 or t = π
2sin²(t) - cos(t) = 1
Using sin²(t) = 1-cos²(t)
2(1-cos²(t)) - cos(t)=1
Distributing the 2:
2-2cos²(t) - cos(t) = 1
Now we have a quadratic
-2cos²(t)-cos(t) + 1 = 0
Factor (multiply by -1 to simplify factoring
2cos²(t) + cos(t) - 1 = 0
(2cos(t)-1)(cos(t)+1) = 0
2cos(t)-1 = 0 OR cos(t)+1 = 0
cos(t) = 1/2 or cos(t) = -1
t = π/3 or t = 5π/3 or t = π

73.110.32.80.121.116.104.111.110.44.95.9.question_text: In Python,__________ is a blueprint for how something should be defined, but it doesn’t contain any data. On the other hand, _________ is an object built from a class, and it contains real data.
73.110.32.80.121.116.104.111.110.44.95.9.answer_text:
a class
an instance 

A class is a blueprint for how something should be defined. It doesn’t actually contain any data. For example, a Dog class may specify that a name and an age are necessary for defining a dog, but it doesn’t contain the name or age of any specific dog.

On the other hand, an instance is an object that’s built from a class and contains real data. An instance of a Dog class isn’t a blueprint anymore. It’s an actual dog with a name, like Frieda, who’s four years old.

subject: history
87.104.111.32.105.110.118.101.110.116.10.question_text: Who invented the rotary printing press? When did they invent it?
87.104.111.32.105.110.118.101.110.116.10.answer_text: By the mid 19th century Richard M. Hoe of New York had perfected a power-driven cylinder press in which a large central cylinder carrying the type successively printed on the paper of four impression cylinders, producing 8000 sheets an hour in 2,000 revolutions. The rotary press came to dominate the high-speed newspaper field, but the flatbed press, having a flat bed to hold the type and either a reciprocating platen or a cylinder to hold the paper, continued to be used for job printing.

subject: memory_science
119.104.97.116.32.100.111.101.115.32.101.answer_text:
The processes of the interaction between engram and retrieval cues 

Put another way, the engram is permissive or necessary for memory but does not suffice for its materialization. A memory emerges when the engram interacts with retrieval cues or information derived from particular environmental conditions,a process which Semon termed “ecphory”. The product of this retrieval–engram interaction is a memory. Without retrieval, there is only the engram, and even its existence is inferred from memory’s emergence.

subject: memory_science
87.104.101.110.32.100.111.101.115.32.109.question_text: When does memory exist according to Dudai?
87.104.101.110.32.100.111.101.115.32.109.answer_text:
Memory does not exist until it is recovered 

"We can more accurately say that memory "effectively" only exists when recovered. In absence of retrieval the memory is of null effect. The memory itself is not a constant and is altered when retrieved, so the encoded memory stored is rarely the retrieved memory."

subject: memory_science
84.104.101.32.98.111.111.107.32.105.110..question_text: The book in the library analogy, when reinforcing the idea that memory does not exist until its recovered, says what?
84.104.101.32.98.111.111.107.32.105.110..answer_text:
If you look for a book in a library and can't find it, does it exist? In effect the book does not exist until you find it. Reference to a book does not mean the book exists in the library.

Description: 
The general point that a memory does not exist until it is recovered can be illustrated with a simple analogy of looking for a book in a library. Suppose you think a particular book is in the library. You go to the shelf where you believe it was located and it is not there. Does the book exist in the library or not? One possibility is that you looked in the wrong place. You now look up its call number but you discover that the book is not at the location the call number speciﬁed. There are now two possibilities: the book is somewhere in the library but you cannot ﬁnd it, or the book has disappeared. For all intents and purposes, as long as you do not recover the book, you cannot know whether it exists. Saying it is there because there is a record that it possibly existed once (the call number) does not solve the problem, but begs the question.

87.104.97.116.32.105.115.32.116.104.101..answer_text:
The identification of the specific information that is encoded in neural circuits and of the changes that occur in this information with learning.

Details:
This as opposed to: (a) types of neural mechanisms, including plasticity mechanisms, which serve as biological vehicles (‘tools of neural syntax’) to embody representations (‘tokens of neural semantics’);(b) changes that are a manifestation of biological house-keeping and homeostasis. Identification of the relevant codes and representations(but see below) could permit at the end of the day molecular, cellular and circuit data to be related to behaviorally relevant memory mechanisms,and distinguish memory-related changes from non-memory-related changes.

65.32.99.101.108.108.32.117.115.101.32.1.question_text: A cell uses what to accelerate chemical reactions enabling its metabolic machinery to operate?
65.32.99.101.108.108.32.117.115.101.32.1.answer_text:
Enzymes

Enzymes are catalysts, chemicals that speed up chemical reactions. The conditions inside most cells do not allow reactions to proceed quickly enough to support life. For example, temperatures inside organisms are not hot enough for chemical reactions to take place quickly. Enzymes increase the rates of biological chemical reactions.

subject: biology

84.104.101.32.116.119.111.32.115.121.115.question_text: The two systems of the body that are responsible for removing waste are the digestive system and the circulatory system? True or False?
84.104.101.32.116.119.111.32.115.121.115.answer_text:
False,

The two primary systems responsible for removing waste from the body are the digestive system and the excretory system (which includes the urinary system). The digestive system eliminates solid waste through the intestines, while the excretory system removes liquid waste and toxins through the kidneys and bladder. The circulatory system plays a role in transporting waste products to be filtered, but it is not directly responsible for waste removal.

68.78.65.32.101.120.105.115.116.115.32.1.answer_text:

DNA is made of two strands of nucleotides (basic units of DNA). The DNA molecule looks like a twisted ladder and is referred to as a double helix.  Each nucleotide contains a sugar molecule and a phosphate group, along with a nitrogen base. The sugar molecules and phosphate groups make up the sides of the ladder, and the nitrogen bases make up the “steps” of the ladder.

84.104.101.32.112.114.101.100.111.109.10.question_text: The predominant type of neurons found in the human brain are sensory neurons. True or False?
84.104.101.32.112.114.101.100.111.109.10.answer_text:
False, 

The predominant type of neurons in the human brain are interneurons. These neurons connect other neurons within the brain and spinal cord, facilitating communication between sensory and motor neurons. While sensory neurons are important for transmitting information from sensory receptors to the central nervous system, they are not the most numerous type of neurons in the brain. Motor neurons, which send signals from the central nervous system to muscles, are also present but less numerous than interneurons.

84.114.117.101.32.111.114.32.70.97.108.1.question_text: True or False, Digestion is a form of anabolism?
84.114.117.101.32.111.114.32.70.97.108.1.answer_text:
False, 

Digestion is primarily a form of catabolism, not anabolism.

Catabolism refers to the breakdown of complex molecules into simpler ones, releasing energy in the process. Digestion involves breaking down food into smaller molecules (like amino acids and sugars) that can be absorbed and utilized by the body.

Anabolism, on the other hand, refers to the building up of complex molecules from simpler ones, which requires energy. This includes processes like protein synthesis and the formation of new cells.

So, digestion is associated with breaking down food, making it a catabolic process.

A gene is the basic unit of heredity. Genes are made of DNA sequences and act as instructions for making proteins (although not all genes make proteins). Genes are found on chromosomes. Chromosomes are located in a cell's nucleus.

73.110.32.97.32.101.117.107.97.114.121.1.question_text: In a eukaryotic (animal) cell, what is the proper term for the area within the cell which house's the DNA?
73.110.32.97.32.101.117.107.97.114.121.1.answer_text:
cell nucleus 

In a eukaryotic (animal) cell, the area that houses the DNA is called the nucleus. The nucleus is a membrane-bound organelle that contains the cell's genetic material organized into chromosomes. It plays a crucial role in regulating gene expression and mediating the replication of DNA during the cell cycle.

subject: biology
65.32.99.101.108.108.32.109.101.109.98.1.question_text: A cell membrane is permeable, semipermeable or non-permeable?
65.32.99.101.108.108.32.109.101.109.98.1.answer_text:
semi-permeable 

A cell membrane is a thin layer of molecules that surrounds the cell. Its function is to allow certain materials to enter the cell, yet, keep harmful materials out. In order to do this, the cell controls which substances can enter the cell through the membrane. The cell membrane is selectively permeable (or semipermeable), which means that certain molecules, such as oxygen and water, are permitted to enter the cell. Molecules that are not recognized as helpful are not able to pass through the membrane.

72.111.119.32.100.105.100.32.69.114.97.1.question_text: How did Eratothenes measure the Earth?
72.111.119.32.100.105.100.32.69.114.97.1.answer_text:
At Syene Egypt (near modern Aswan), sunlight struck the bottom of a vertical well at noon. Eratosthenes observes the shadow of a column made and saw the Sun was not directly overhead, but cast a shadow of about 7° (1/50th of a circle)

Using the distance between the two objects (5000 stadia), multiplied by 50 gives us the approximate 250,000 stadia circumference of the Earth.

83.111.109.101.32.112.104.105.108.111.11.question_text: Some philosophers in ancient times, such as Aristarchus of Samos, suggested that Earth was moving around the Sun. Why was this theory rejected?
83.111.109.101.32.112.104.105.108.111.11.answer_text: The theory suggested either that Earth was not moving or that the stars had to be so tremendously far away that the parallax shift was immeasurably small. A cosmos of such enormous extent required a leap of imagination that most ancient philosophers were not prepared to make, so they retreated to the safety of the Earth-centered view, which would dominate Western thinking for nearly two millennia.

87.114.105.116.101.32.97.32.112.121.116..question_text: Write a python script that checks whether a datetime object is within 24 hours of date.now()
87.114.105.116.101.32.97.32.112.121.116..answer_text:
def within_twenty_four_hours(datetime_object):
    now = datetime.now()
    time_delta = now - datetime_object
    return abs(time_delta.total_seconds()) <= 24 * 3600

subject: history
2024-11-18 16:53:56.708381_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: Where was the first major battle of World War I?
2024-11-18 16:53:56.708381_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The first major battle of WWI took place at Marne and lasted from September 6-10, 1914. This site is located in the northwest of France, about 50 miles from Paris.
2024-11-18 16:53:56.708381_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 16:55:31.122782_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: World War 1 lasted a lot longer than the parties involved had anticipated, so that meant more men would have to join to keep the forces stocked. How many people would enlist in total?
2024-11-18 16:55:31.122782_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: Over the four years that the battle raged, 65 million individuals from both sides would join the fight.
2024-11-18 16:55:31.122782_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 16:56:40.985702_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: Both sides had their coalition names. On one side, you had the Central Powers, and on the other, you had the Allied Powers. Three countries that were part of the Allied Forces were also known under another name. What was that name?
2024-11-18 16:56:40.985702_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The three-country unit was known as the Triple Entente.
2024-11-18 16:56:40.985702_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 16:58:31.700767_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: Which countries were a part of the Triple Entente?
2024-11-18 16:58:31.700767_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text:
he UK, France, and Russia. 
(By the way, the Central Powers were also known as the Triple Alliance and consisted of Germany, Italy, and Austria-Hungary.)
2024-11-18 16:58:31.700767_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 17:00:06.145951_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: World War I would eventually be taken up by a majority of the powers of the world, but who declared war first?
2024-11-18 17:00:06.145951_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: After the assassination of the Austro-Hungarian heir in Sarajevo, the first declaration came from the Austro-Hungarian empire.
2024-11-18 17:00:06.145951_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 17:01:22.546906_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: There were few places more dangerous in WWI than the area between the trenches. It was so deadly that troops came up with a name for it. What did the soldiers call that area?
2024-11-18 17:01:22.546906_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text:
No Man's Land

The name didn’t have to be smart, just accurate. Soldiers called it “No Man’s Land.”
2024-11-18 17:01:22.546906_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 17:02:51.978706_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: As a result of World War One ending, a mighty governing body formed to make sure a conflict of this magnitude wouldn’t happen again. What was the name of this group?
2024-11-18 17:02:51.978706_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text:
League of Nations:

After the Treaty of Versailles, a global ruling organization known as the League of Nations was formed. This governing body was invented to keep things like war from happening.
2024-11-18 17:02:51.978706_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 17:03:47.908381_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: How long was the League of Nations active?
2024-11-18 17:03:47.908381_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The League of Nations would be active from 1919 until 1946. At this point, it dissolved, and the UN filled its place in the world.
2024-11-18 17:03:47.908381_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 17:05:32.697549_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: Many factors led the world to war, but these main political concepts played the most prominent roles. What were the three movements that helped bring the world to the precipice of war?
2024-11-18 17:05:32.697549_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The three social and political movements were imperialism, militarism, and nationalism.
2024-11-18 17:05:32.697549_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 17:08:15.461326_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: Once the Bolsheviks took charge in Russia, the leader of that party felt it was time to pull his troops back from the eastern front of the war. Which leader made this decision?
2024-11-18 17:08:15.461326_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: In 1917, Lenin began to pull the Russian troops back to the mother country as he looked to start building a communist nation and wanted to have all his people there to help him do it. There were remnants of the White Army that needed to be taken care of before the new government could be formed as well.
2024-11-18 17:08:15.461326_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 17:33:04.728905_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: The World War One Truce stopped the fighting on what day in history?
2024-11-18 17:33:04.728905_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The truce was the first step in ending the war and went into effect on November 11, 1918. It would be a couple of months before the actual treaty would be signed, but this was a good start in ending the bloodshed
2024-11-18 17:33:04.728905_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 17:35:09.567629_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: When talking about the causes of the war, many people use the acronym MAIN. What does it stand for?
2024-11-18 17:35:09.567629_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: It stands for militarism, alliances, imperialism, and nationalism.
2024-11-18 17:35:09.567629_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 17:36:55.065866_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: What neutral country did Germany march through at the beginning of world war one?
2024-11-18 17:36:55.065866_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text:
Belgium

Germany’s move was intended to be in France, but before they got to France, they had to march through Belgium. This country had been neutral, and once Germany began mobilizing through its land, Britain soon realized they would be needed in the effort to stop Germany’s aggression.
2024-11-18 17:36:55.065866_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 17:39:24.970734_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: Why did Germany decide to attack both France and Russia?
2024-11-18 17:39:24.970734_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: It was all about alliances. The Russians did not support Austria-Hungary’s annexation of Serbia and instead backed the Serbian nationalists. After this decision, Russia became enemies with Austria-Hungary, who had a strong alliance with Germany. So, the minute the Russian troops moved to the border, Germany moved to help their ally out. Russia also had an agreement with France, and the German leadership knew that once Germany and Austria-Hungary moved on Russia, the French were obliged to send troops to help their comrades out. So as a stop-gap measure, the Germans sent troops to cut off the French’s capability to send their troops. This led to Germany ending up fighting a two-front war.
2024-11-18 17:39:24.970734_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 17:42:25.801761_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: Why did Germany decide to attack both France and Russia?
2024-11-18 17:42:25.801761_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: It was all about alliances. The Russians did not support Austria-Hungary’s annexation of Serbia and instead backed the Serbian nationalists. After this decision, Russia became enemies with Austria-Hungary, who had a strong alliance with Germany. So, the minute the Russian troops moved to the border, Germany moved to help their ally out. Russia also had an agreement with France, and the German leadership knew that once Germany and Austria-Hungary moved on Russia, the French were obliged to send troops to help their comrades out. So as a stop-gap measure, the Germans sent troops to cut off the French’s capability to send their troops. This led to Germany ending up fighting a two-front war.
2024-11-18 17:42:25.801761_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 17:44:00.121936_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: Which country did Germany declare war on first, Russia or France?
2024-11-18 17:44:00.121936_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: Germany declared war on Russia first. On August 1, 1914, Germany would make the pronouncement that they would be going to war with Russia. Two days later, on August 3, 1914, they extended the same proclamation to France.
2024-11-18 17:44:00.121936_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 17:46:54.425833_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, When did the British Expeditionary Force (BEF) land in France?
2024-11-18 17:46:54.425833_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The BEF first set foot on French soil on August 7, 1914. Though smaller than the French Army, the British forces were better trained.
2024-11-18 17:46:54.425833_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 17:48:35.162601_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: Turkey would join WW I after it had already begun. When did they enter the war, and on what side?
2024-11-18 17:48:35.162601_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: Turkey and the Ottoman Empire had signed into a treaty in 1914, with Germany as the newly-formed German State that helped them greatly with their military building efforts. On October 29, 1914, Turkey would join the war efforts on the side of Germany. Turkey felt they had to help and that by joining in with the German efforts, they could potentially destabilize the control of the French and British in other Muslim countries.
2024-11-18 17:48:35.162601_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 17:50:04.342518_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War I, The Battle of the Falkland Islands was a significant win for the British naval forces. What date did this battle occur?
2024-11-18 17:50:04.342518_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The Battle of the Falkland Islands took place on December 8, 1914
2024-11-18 17:50:04.342518_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 17:51:11.201317_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, how many German and British soldiers were lost at the Battle of the Falkland Islands?
2024-11-18 17:51:11.201317_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: This battle was a decisive victory, where the British only lost ten men, whereas the Germans lost 2,000 men.
2024-11-18 17:51:11.201317_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 17:53:37.321782_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: The Lusitania was a passenger ship that was sunk and played a significant role in the U.S. joining the war. Where was it sunk?
2024-11-18 17:53:37.321782_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: On May 7, 1915, the RMS Lusitania was attacked by the German U-boat U-28 off the southern coast of Ireland.
2024-11-18 17:53:37.321782_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 17:54:41.786269_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: How many people were lost when the Lusitania sank?
2024-11-18 17:54:41.786269_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: When the ship sank, 1,198 passengers went down with it, 100 of those being citizens of America.
2024-11-18 17:54:41.786269_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 17:55:57.864668_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, When and why did Kaiser Wilhelm II abdicate the German throne?
2024-11-18 17:55:57.864668_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: There was a wide swath of unrest spreading across the German state. With a naval mutiny having just been executed and that growing unrest, the political leaders of Germany were convinced to push for the abdication. So, on November 9, 1918, Kaiser Wilhelm II left the throne and fled to the Netherlands.
2024-11-18 17:55:57.864668_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 17:57:50.590173_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: What was the name of the general of the French Sixth Army that led the troops to success at the First Battle of Marne, during WWI?
2024-11-18 17:57:50.590173_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: General Michel-Joseph Maunoury led the Sixth Army Division. This wartime hero had been retired for several years when the French Army called him back to service. In August of 1914, the 67-year-old artillery officer would find his way back to the battlefield and lead the Sixth Army to victory in the First Battle of Marne.
2024-11-18 17:57:50.590173_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 17:59:43.753008_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, After being driven back, where did the German Army retreat after the First Battle of Marne?
2024-11-18 17:59:43.753008_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The battle pushed the German forces back to the northern edge of the Aisne River.
2024-11-18 17:59:43.753008_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:00:52.366081_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: The defeat at the Battle of Marne set into motion a series of flanking maneuvers on both sides. What were these maneuvers called?
2024-11-18 18:00:52.366081_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: “Race to the Sea.” When the Germans retreated to the Aisne river, they began trying to use trenches to outflank their enemy. To counteract this, the other side started doing this themselves. This back and forth led to a series of trenches all linked together and protected at the top by barb wire, which was the real beginning of trench warfare during WWI.
2024-11-18 18:00:52.366081_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:01:48.645866_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, What was the German Army’s big mistake at the Battle of Marne?
2024-11-18 18:01:48.645866_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The leader of the German forces moved his troops in the wrong direction. This maneuver created a hole in the German defensive line and allowed the French and British to separate the forces, weakening their ability to fight effectively.
2024-11-18 18:01:48.645866_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:03:23.963005_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, Who made the mistake that caused the germans to lose the First Battle of Marne?
2024-11-18 18:03:23.963005_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The breach in the German Army line came when General Alexander von Kluck moved his troops north instead of west.
2024-11-18 18:03:23.963005_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:04:13.349547_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, What were some of the reasons for the German defeat at the First Battle of Marne?
2024-11-18 18:04:13.349547_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: There were many reasons that the German Army wasn’t at full strength, starting with the movement of 11 different divisions away from the line looking to advance and take over Paris. These divisions dispersed to other areas like Belgium and East Prussia as reinforcement for the conflicts going on in these regions. The German Army had also marched 150 miles without stopping, and the troops were exhausted. While they marched, they also saw many battles, which led to fatigue. The French Army was also not taking any chances and had begun demolishing bridges and railways, so that would mean fewer supplies as well as further to travel for the Germans. The Germans underestimated the French, which was a severe tactical miscalculation.
2024-11-18 18:04:13.349547_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:05:02.150555_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, When was the First Battle of Marne fought?
2024-11-18 18:05:02.150555_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The battle began in northeastern France, just 30 miles from Paris on September 6, 1914. Within the six days of the First Battle of Marne, the world would see many casualties and introduce some innovations. The battle would come to an end on September 12, 1914.
2024-11-18 18:05:02.150555_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:05:59.289789_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, How many ships and sailors participated in the historic naval Battle at Jutland?
2024-11-18 18:05:59.289789_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: Altogether, there were over 250 ships and 100,000 sailors involved in the epic military Battle at Jutland.
2024-11-18 18:05:59.289789_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:07:45.088230_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, The Battle of Jutland was fought where?
2024-11-18 18:07:45.088230_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The Battle of Jutland was the most massive naval battle in history and took place off the North Sea coast of Denmark.
2024-11-18 18:07:45.088230_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:09:13.617952_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, When did the Battle of Jutland start and end?
2024-11-18 18:09:13.617952_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The battle began on May 31, 1916, and ended just one day later on June 1, 1916.
2024-11-18 18:09:13.617952_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:10:17.587812_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, Who were the admirals on each side of the Battle of Jutland?
2024-11-18 18:10:17.587812_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: Admiral John Jellicoe commanded the British fleet that saw action in the Battle of Jutland. For the opposition, the German fleet was led by Admiral Reinhard Scheer.
2024-11-18 18:10:17.587812_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:11:18.674212_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, When did the Battle of Verdun take place?
2024-11-18 18:11:18.674212_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The first barrage of German artillery began on February 21, 1916. The battle would last for almost a year and finally came to its conclusion on December 18, 1916.
2024-11-18 18:11:18.674212_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:12:24.564724_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, The Battle of Verdun claimed how many lives?
2024-11-18 18:12:24.564724_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: Though the numbers are not wholly accurate, the estimate of lives lost in the 11-month battle comes to 400,000 on the French side and 350,000 on the German side.
2024-11-18 18:12:24.564724_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:14:25.519490_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, Who was the German General who led the Spring Offensive?
2024-11-18 18:14:25.519490_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The German mastermind behind the Spring Offensive was General Erich Ludendorff.
2024-11-18 18:14:25.519490_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:15:09.706415_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, How signficant was the battlefront of the Spring Offensive?
2024-11-18 18:15:09.706415_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: Ludendorff chose a large swath of land to execute his Spring Offensive. A total of approximately 50 miles of the western front’s Allied defense line would be the German's last great push.
2024-11-18 18:15:09.706415_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:15:49.006233_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, What were the two goals that General Ludendorff had for the Spring Offensive?
2024-11-18 18:15:49.006233_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: Ludendorff intended to use psychological warfare and tactics to eliminate the British part of the resistance army. To do this, the central part of the plan was to drive the German Army between the French and British troops. Then turn north to deal with the less experienced British Army, thereby eliminating the French reinforcements.
2024-11-18 18:15:49.006233_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:16:27.082955_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, Who was the French commander at the Battle of Amiens?
2024-11-18 18:16:27.082955_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The man who spearheaded the Battle of Amiens for the French was General Ferdinand Foch.
2024-11-18 18:16:27.082955_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:17:36.183295_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, What nations played a role in the Battle of Amiens as part of the Fourth Army?
2024-11-18 18:17:36.183295_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: To bolster Rawlinson’s Army, he added infantry divisions from Britain, Canada, Australia, and America.
2024-11-18 18:17:36.183295_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:18:24.075259_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, The Gallipoli Campaign, like all other battles, has been called many things. What are two different names for this tragic Allied loss?
2024-11-18 18:18:24.075259_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: This campaign is also known as the Battle of Gallipoli and the Dardanelles Campaign.
2024-11-18 18:18:24.075259_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:19:22.517625_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: Relating to World War One, ANZAC stands for?
2024-11-18 18:19:22.517625_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: One of the pivotal forces in the execution of the Gallipoli Campaign was the ANZACs. This force was the Australian and New Zealand Army Corps – ANZAC.
2024-11-18 18:19:22.517625_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:20:17.197974_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, What was the main objective of the Gallipoli Campaign?
2024-11-18 18:20:17.197974_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: Gallipoli was undertaken after Grand Duke Nicholas asked for help. The British naval command had reason to execute a campaign they had wanted to do for years. Not only would they be helping their Russian allies, but they would also be able to control the sea route from Europe to Russia.
2024-11-18 18:20:17.197974_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:21:14.235783_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, Why did the Russian Grand Duke Nicholas ask for help from Britain in 1915?
2024-11-18 18:21:14.235783_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The Russians needed a way to end the involvement of the Turkish forces in the Caucus Mountain area so they would only have to fight foes on one side.
2024-11-18 18:21:14.235783_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:22:03.686014_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, What were the two beachheads established by the Allied Forces on the Gallipoli Peninsula?
2024-11-18 18:22:03.686014_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The beachhead established on the southern tip of the peninsula was called Helles. Though later it would be renamed in honor of the fallen ANZAC soldiers, ANZAC Cove. The second beachhead was on the Aegean, and it was called Gaba Tepe.
2024-11-18 18:22:03.686014_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:22:37.792782_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, When was the evacuation of Gallipoli ordered?
2024-11-18 18:22:37.792782_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The initial order to begin evacuation came down on December 7, 1915.
2024-11-18 18:22:37.792782_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:24:30.203146_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, How long did the evacuation of Gallipoli take?
2024-11-18 18:24:30.203146_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The last troops did not leave until January 9, 1916.
2024-11-18 18:24:30.203146_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:25:44.716337_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, What were the results of the Battle of Megiddo for the Ottoman forces and Empire?
2024-11-18 18:25:44.716337_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The Ottoman's loss at Megiddo sealed the fate of the 600-year reign of this mighty empire. They were forced to sign an armistice on October 30. In this armistice, they agreed to the partition of their empire, giving lands back to previously-conquered places. Though it would take a few years for the final dissolution of empire (it wouldn’t be until 1923, until it officially ended) altogether, this indeed was the final blow.
2024-11-18 18:25:44.716337_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:27:13.353678_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, Where and when did the "Red Baron" die?
2024-11-18 18:27:13.353678_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The “Red Baron” had been given his fighting squadron and was engaging the enemy in April 1918. As he swooped low to follow an enemy plane, he was taken by surprise by a machine gunner on the ground. He would take a bullet and lose control of his plane, nose-diving into the ground and meeting his end.
2024-11-18 18:27:13.353678_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:27:58.118182_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: Relating to World War One, Who was Edith Cavell?
2024-11-18 18:27:58.118182_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: Edith Cavell, a British nurse, formerly from the Norfolk area of England, became a hero to the Belgian Resistance. She helped Allied troops escape occupied-Belgium through a path to the neutral Netherlands and back to England.
2024-11-18 18:27:58.118182_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:28:54.401502_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, Where was the Lusitania bound for when it launched?
2024-11-18 18:28:54.401502_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The Lusitania launched from Liverpool and was headed to New York.
2024-11-18 18:28:54.401502_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:29:54.502378_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: What former president of the U.S.A. demanded retaliation after the sinking of the Lusitania?
2024-11-18 18:29:54.502378_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: Woodrow Wilson decided not to instigate any ill feelings with the German state when this happened, but former President Teddy Roosevelt felt this was not the right move and called for immediate retaliation for the American lives lost.
2024-11-18 18:29:54.502378_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:31:20.532760_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, When was the first Zeppelin raid on London executed?
2024-11-18 18:31:20.532760_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: On May 31, 1915, the Germans used the Thames River and its lights to guide them toward their most significant target yet, London. The doors opened that night and dropped 90 incendiary bombs and 30 grenades onto the unsuspecting people of London.
2024-11-18 18:31:20.532760_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:32:40.009462_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: War is deadly, but there was another fatal enemy during WW I. What caused a third of the military deaths during the war?
2024-11-18 18:32:40.009462_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: It wasn’t bullets or tanks. No, the outbreak of the Spanish Influenza took its toll on the military men and women of the Great War. There were three primary outbreaks, two of which were during the war.
2024-11-18 18:32:40.009462_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:33:58.239396_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: What well-known moniker did the German pilot, Manfred von Richthofen, go by?
2024-11-18 18:33:58.239396_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The illustrious German pilot is better known as the Red Baron.
2024-11-18 18:33:58.239396_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:34:36.302562_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, how many enemy planes did the "Red Baron" shoot down?
2024-11-18 18:34:36.302562_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: In his flying career, he shot down a whopping 80 British and French planes.
2024-11-18 18:34:36.302562_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:35:11.075780_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, Which general led the American Army on the western front?
2024-11-18 18:35:11.075780_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: General John “Black Jack” Pershing was the commander of the American Expeditionary Forces (AEF) that fought alongside the French and British troops to fend off the advancing German empire.
2024-11-18 18:35:11.075780_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:36:16.092119_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: World War I was known by a few different names. What were the two main ones?
2024-11-18 18:36:16.092119_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: The war would be dubbed World War I, but it was also known as “The War to End All Wars” and “The Kaiser’s War.”
2024-11-18 18:36:16.092119_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:37:20.065101_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, The U.S. tried to keep from entering the war, but eventually, they would see that they needed to help their allies. What was the date the U.S. finally joined the conflict?
2024-11-18 18:37:20.065101_47d39d7b-37ff.answer_text: At first, President Wilson stated that the U.S. would stay neutral in the conflict. However, Germany’s announcement that they were going to use submarines without prejudice to keep merchant ships from entering Britain became a problem. This decree would lead to the sinking of the Lusitania , which had 128 American citizens on board. On top of that, the “Zimmermann Telegram” was intercepted, and its content threatened U.S. borders. This telegraph insinuated that Germany would ally with Mexico. At that point, Wilson had no alternative, and he sent America to the war on April 6, 1917.
2024-11-18 18:37:20.065101_47d39d7b-37ff.author: 47d39d7b-37ff-461b-aeec-ca52e36c101d

subject: history
2024-11-18 18:38:21.508679_47d39d7b-37ff.question_text: During World War One, Russia fought a two-sided war while the war ensued. On their western border, they were crucial components in the World War, but in 1917, they also began to experience their internal conflict. What was this conflict?
